title: Force and Torque on Current Carrying Conductor

concepts.title: Magnetic Force on Current Carrying Conductor
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Force on Current Carrying Conductor 

 

A charged particle in motion experiences a magnetic force in a magnetic field. Similarly, a current carrying wire also experiences a force when placed in a magnetic field. This follows from the fact that the current is a collection of many charged particles in motion. Hence, the resultant force exerted by the field on the wire is the vector sum of the individual forces exerted on all the charged particles making up the current. The force exerted on the particles is transmitted to the wire when the particles collide with the atoms making up the wire.

Suppose a conducting wire carrying a current $i$, is placed in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$. The length of the wire is 1 and area of cross-section is A. The free electrons drift with a speed ${{\text{v}}_{\text{d}}}$ opposite to the direction of current. The magnetic force exerted on the electron is,

$\text{d}{{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=-\text{e}\left( \overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{\text{d}}}}\times \vec{B} \right)$

If $\text{n}$ be the number of free electrons per unit volume of the wire, then total number of electrons in volume $\text{Al}$ of the wire are, $\text{nAl}$. Therefore, total force on the wire is,

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=-\text{e}\left( \text{nAl} \right)\left( \overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{\text{d}}}}\times \vec{B} \right)$

If we denote the length 1 along the direction of the current by $\vec{l}$, then the above equation becomes.

where $neA{{v}_{d}}=\text{i}$

Following points are worth noting regarding, the above expression:

1.Magnitude of ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}$ is, ${{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{ilBsin}\theta $, here $\theta $ is the angle between $\vec{l}$ and $\vec{B}\cdot {{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}$ is zero for $\theta =0$ or 180 and maximum for $\theta =90$.

2.Here $\vec{l}$ is a vector that points in the direction of the current $i$ and has a magnitude equal to the length.

3.The above expression applies only to a straight segment of wire in a uniform magnetic field.

4.For the magnetic force on an arbitrarily shaped wire segment, let us consider the magnetic force exerted on a small segment of vector length $\text{d}\vec{l}$.

$\text{d}{{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{i}\left( \text{d}\vec{l}\times \vec{B} \right)$ …(ii)

To calculate the total force ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}$ acting on their wire shown in figure, we integrate over the length of the wire.

${{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{i}\mathop{\int }_{\text{A}}^{\text{D}}\left( \text{d}\vec{l}\times \vec{B} \right)$ …(iii)

Now let us consider two special cases involving Eq. (iii). In both cases, the magnetic field is taken to be constant in magnitude and direction.

Case 1 . A curved wire ACD as shown in Fig. (a) carries a current $\text{i}$ and is located in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$. Because the field is uniform, we can take $\text{\vec{B}}$ outside the integral in Eq. (iii) and we obtain.

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{i}\left( \mathop{\int }_{\text{A}}^{\text{D}}\overrightarrow{\text{dl}} \right)\times \vec{B}$ …(iv)

But the quantity $\int _{A}^{\text{D}}\overrightarrow{\text{dl}}$ represents the vector sum of all length elements from A to D. From the polygon law of vector addition, the sum equals the vector directed from A to D. Thus,

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{i}\left( \vec{l}\times \vec{B} \right)$

or we can write, ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{ACD}}}={{\vec{F}}_{\text{AD}}}=\text{i}\left( \overrightarrow{\text{AD}}\times \vec{B} \right)$ in uniform field.

Case 2. An arbitrarily shaped closed loop carrying a current $\text{i}$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field as shown in Fig. (b). We can again express the force acting on the loop in the form of Eq. (iv), but this time we must take the vector sum of the length elements $\overrightarrow{\text{dl}}$ over the entire loop,

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{i}\left( \oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\overrightarrow{\text{dl}} \right)\times \vec{B}$

Because the set of length elements forms a closed polygon, the vector sum must be zero. $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}=0$

 

Thus, the net magnetic force acting on any closed current loop in a uniform magnetic field is zero.

5.The direction of ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{m}}}$ can be given by Fleming's left-hand rule According to this rule, the forefinger, the central finger, and the thumb of the left hand are stretched in such a way that they are mutually perpendicular to each other. If the central finger shows the direction of current (or $\vec{l})$ and forefinger shows the direction of magnetic field $\left( {\vec{B}} \right)$, then the thumb will give the direction of magnetic force $\left( {{{\vec{F}}}_{\text{m}}} \right)$.

6.The point of application of magnetic force is taken to be center of Mass of the body.

concepts.title: Potential Energy and Work Done by Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Potential Energy 

When the magnetic moment is perpendicular to the loop is said to be in the zero-energy state.

When the loop rotates due to the torque given by magnetic field. Then the rotation K.E. of the loop increases and the P.E. decreases.

Calculating the work done by Magnetic Field

${{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}=-\int \tau \cdot \text{d}\theta $

$=-\mathop{\int }_{\frac{\pi }{2}}^{\theta }\text{MBsin}\theta \text{d}\theta $

${{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}=\text{MBcos}\theta $

Thus, the potential energy will decrease by an amount MB $\text{cos}\theta $, Therefore.

$\text{U}=-\text{MBcos}\theta $

Or \(\mathrm{U}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}\)

 

Graph of $U$ v/s $\theta $ :

When $\vec{M}$ and $\vec{B}$ are in the same direction then the loop is in stable equilibrium state. And when they are in opposite direction then the loop is in unstable equilibrium state.
